title.en: SIM Swap Fraud — Signs, Emergency Response and Getting Your Money Back

answerBox.en: In SIM swap fraud, criminals get a duplicate of your SIM and receive your OTPs — the tell-tale sign is your phone suddenly showing no network for hours. Act in minutes: call your telecom operator from another phone to block the duplicate, call bank hotlines to freeze accounts, then 1930 for any money moved. The RBI zero-liability rules apply — these are unauthorised transactions.

en.label: Block the duplicate SIM
en.startsFrom: call the operator from any phone

en.title: Recognise it fast
en.summary: Dead network + OTP/debit alerts on email = swap in progress.
en.detail: A SIM swap looks like: **no network for hours** where others have coverage, "SIM registration failed", or a sudden restart demanding activation — often preceded by phishing calls posing as telecom "KYC upgrade". Meanwhile debit alerts pile up on your **email** (which fraudsters may also try to lock). Any unexplained network death when others have signal deserves the emergency drill, not a wait for "network to come back".

en.title: Kill the duplicate SIM, then freeze money
en.summary: Operator first (it stops the OTP tap), banks second.
en.detail: From any phone: call your **operator's helpline** (Airtel 121/198, Jio 199, Vi 199) and report an unauthorised SIM swap — demand immediate **blocking of the duplicate** and reissue to you with in-store ID verification. Then bank hotlines/apps: **freeze cards, UPI and net-banking**; change email passwords and enable app-based 2FA (not SMS). Every minute of the duplicate SIM's life is an OTP the fraudster receives.

en.title: 1930 + cybercrime portal + bank letter
en.summary: The standard financial-fraud reporting chain applies.
en.detail: For money already moved: **1930** immediately (freeze attempt), complaint on [cybercrime.gov.in](https://cybercrime.gov.in), and a **written bank complaint within 3 working days** — SIM-swap debits are unauthorised electronic transactions squarely inside [RBI's zero-liability circular](https://www.rbi.org.in/Scripts/NotificationUser.aspx?Id=11040). SIM-swap fraud implicates cheating by personation — **Section 419/420 IPC (now Sections 319/318 BNS)** — and IT Act Sections 66C (identity theft) and 66D. Ask the operator in writing how the duplicate was issued: KYC lapses there support both your bank claim and liability of the telco.

en.title: Recover and harden
en.summary: Ombudsman escalations both ways; kill SMS-OTP dependence.
en.detail: Bank stalling? **RBI Ombudsman** at cms.rbi.org.in after 30 days. Operator stonewalling on how the swap happened? **Telecom consumer grievance** → appellate authority, and the DoT's Sanchar Saathi portal ([sancharsaathi.gov.in](https://sancharsaathi.gov.in)) to check/block connections issued in your name. Hardening: app-based authenticators over SMS OTPs, a SIM PIN, email recovery numbers reviewed, and never sharing the "KYC update" codes telecallers ask for — that code IS the swap authorisation.

en.footnote: Check sancharsaathi.gov.in periodically for SIMs issued against your ID — fraudulent connections in your name are reportable and blockable there even before any fraud hits.

en.q: How do fraudsters get a duplicate of my SIM?
en.a: Social engineering both ends: they phish your ID details and OTPs ("KYC update" calls), then request a swap/duplicate at the operator with forged documents — or trick YOU into initiating the swap by sharing the confirmation code. Operator KYC lapses feature in most cases, which is why their liability is worth pressing in writing.

en.q: Is the bank liable when OTPs went to the fraudster's SIM?
en.a: The transactions are unauthorised — you neither made nor authorised them — which is the zero-liability scenario under RBI's circular if reported within 3 working days. Banks argue "OTP was used"; the answer is that the OTP channel itself was hijacked through no negligence of yours. Ombudsmen have repeatedly sided with SIM-swap victims on this.

en.q: My number was dead for 6 hours and money is gone. Reporting order?
en.a: Operator (kill the duplicate) → banks (freeze) → 1930 (freeze the receiving accounts) → cybercrime portal (written record) → bank's formal complaint letter (zero-liability clock). Then the paper fight: operator's explanation of the swap, bank's response, Ombudsman escalations as needed.

en.q: What is Sanchar Saathi and how does it help?
en.a: DoT's citizen portal: see every mobile connection issued against your identity, flag ones you never took, and block lost/stolen handsets by IMEI. For SIM-swap victims it documents the fraudulent issuance; for everyone it is a free periodic check that prevents identity-based SIM fraud.

en.q: Can I stop relying on SMS OTPs altogether?
en.a: Largely yes: switch banking and email 2FA to authenticator apps/hardware where supported, set UPI limits, and use the bank's app-approve mechanisms instead of SMS where offered. SMS OTP is the single point of failure SIM swap exploits — reduce what one hijacked SIM can unlock.

reviewer: Adv. Sneha Iyer, Bar Council of Tamil Nadu & Puducherry, TN/1187/2013
author: WakilBhai Editorial Team
